mission.title: Install and Configure IBM Cloud Kubernetes Service (IKS)
mission.description: IBM Cloud Kubernetes Service (IKS) is a managed Kubernetes offering that simplifies the deployment and management of Kubernetes clusters on IBM Cloud. It is ideal for users looking for a fully managed solution with integrated tools for monitoring and scaling. Key trade-offs include reliance on IBM Cloud infrastructure and potential costs associated with resource usage.

steps.title: Install the IBM Cloud CLI
steps.description:
This step installs the IBM Cloud Command Line Interface (CLI), which is required to interact with IBM Cloud services.

```bash

curl -sL https://ibm.biz/idt-installer | bash

```

> This command downloads and installs the IBM Cloud CLI.

steps.title: Create a Kubernetes Cluster
steps.description:
This command creates a new Kubernetes cluster on IBM Cloud with the specified version, node count, and region.

```bash

ibmcloud ks cluster-create --name my-cluster --zone us-south-1 --num-workers 2 --kube-version 1.30

```

> Adjust the zone and node count as needed.

steps.title: Set Up Kubeconfig
steps.description:
This command configures your local kubeconfig file to connect to the newly created cluster.

```bash

ibmcloud ks cluster-config --cluster my-cluster

```

> This command updates your kubeconfig to use the new cluster.

steps.title: Verify Cluster Nodes
steps.description:
This command checks the status of the nodes in your Kubernetes cluster to ensure they are running.

```bash

kubectl get nodes

```

> You should see the nodes listed with a 'Ready' status.

steps.title: Post-Install Configuration
steps.description:
This step sets up autoscaling and monitoring for your cluster.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/IBM-Cloud/kube-samples/main/autoscaler.yaml

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/IBM-Cloud/kube-samples/main/monitoring.yaml

```

> This command applies an example autoscaler configuration.
> This command applies an example monitoring configuration.

uninstall.title: Delete the Kubernetes Cluster
uninstall.description:
This command removes the Kubernetes cluster and all associated resources. Be aware that this will result in data loss.

```bash

ibmcloud ks cluster-delete --cluster my-cluster --force

```

> Use --force to skip confirmation prompts.

uninstall.title: Remove Orphaned Resources
uninstall.description:
This command ensures that any orphaned resources such as load balancers and PVCs are cleaned up.

```bash

ibmcloud resource service-instance-delete my-cluster --force

```

> Replace 'my-cluster' with your actual cluster name.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the Kubernetes Cluster
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your data and configurations. This step upgrades the cluster to the specified version.

```bash

ibmcloud ks cluster-update --cluster my-cluster --kube-version 1.31

```

> Replace '1.31' with the desired version.

upgrade.title: Verify Cluster Status
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, verify that the cluster is running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get nodes

```

> Check that all nodes are in 'Ready' state.

upgrade.title: Rollback if Necessary
upgrade.description:
If issues arise after the upgrade, you can roll back to the previous version.

```bash

ibmcloud ks cluster-update --cluster my-cluster --kube-version 1.30

```

> Replace '1.30' with the previous version.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to connect to the server'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This usually indicates that the kubeconfig is not set up correctly or the cluster is not running.

**Fix:**
Run 'ibmcloud ks cluster-config --cluster my-cluster' to set up kubeconfig correctly.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient resources available'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the cluster does not have enough resources to schedule new pods.

**Fix:**
Scale up the cluster by adding more worker nodes using 'ibmcloud ks cluster-update --cluster my-cluster --num-workers <new-count>'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Pod is in CrashLoopBackOff'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that a pod is failing to start due to application errors.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs using 'kubectl logs <pod-name>' to diagnose the issue.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Unauthorized'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the user does not have the necessary permissions to perform the action.

**Fix:**
Ensure your user has the correct IAM roles assigned in IBM Cloud.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new Kubernetes features such as PodSecurity admission and improved resource quotas.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated certain legacy APIs that are no longer recommended for use.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 1.29, review the changes in API usage and update your configurations accordingly.

resolution.summary: You should see the output of 'kubectl get nodes' with all nodes in 'Ready' state and the cluster is operational.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have the IBM Cloud CLI and kubectl installed and configured.
